questions.passage:
The discovery of CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing in 2012 was hailed as a revolution in molecular biology. For the first time, scientists had a tool that could precisely cut DNA at virtually any desired location, guided by a short RNA sequence. The implications seemed limitless: genetic diseases could be corrected, crops engineered for resilience, and evolutionary questions answered through targeted gene manipulation.

Yet the euphoria masked a more complicated reality. Off-target effects — unintended cuts at sites similar but not identical to the target — proved more common than early reports suggested. The cellular repair mechanisms that follow a CRISPR cut can introduce insertions, deletions, or rearrangements that are difficult to predict or control. In 2018, a Chinese researcher announced the birth of twin girls whose embryos had been edited with CRISPR to disable the CCR5 gene, ostensibly to confer HIV resistance. The scientific community responded with near-universal condemnation, not because the technology failed but because it succeeded well enough to be dangerous in reckless hands.

The episode crystallized a tension that runs through the history of biotechnology: the gap between technical capability and the social infrastructure needed to govern it. Regulatory frameworks for gene editing remain fragmented. The United States regulates edited organisms through a patchwork of agencies — FDA, EPA, USDA — whose jurisdictions were designed for earlier technologies. The European Union has classified gene-edited organisms as GMOs subject to stringent pre-market approval, while several South American countries have adopted more permissive approaches that exempt gene-edited organisms from GMO regulations if no foreign DNA is introduced.

The deeper question is whether regulation can keep pace with a technology that is simultaneously becoming cheaper, easier to use, and more powerful. A CRISPR experiment that required a well-funded university laboratory in 2013 can now be performed in a community biohacking space for a few hundred dollars. The democratization of biotechnology is, depending on one's perspective, either the greatest promise of the CRISPR era or its greatest threat.
questions.stem: The author's primary argument about CRISPR technology is that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: gene editing regulation should be uniform across all countries

choices.label: B
choices.text: CRISPR should be banned because its off-target effects make it inherently unsafe

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Chinese researcher's experiment was scientifically justified despite ethical objections

choices.label: D
choices.text: the technical capabilities of CRISPR have outpaced the regulatory and ethical frameworks needed to govern its use responsibly

explanations.A: The passage describes regulatory fragmentation across countries but does not argue for uniformity. It presents the variation as context for the broader argument about governance lagging behind technology.
explanations.B: The author does not advocate banning CRISPR. The passage acknowledges off-target effects as a complication but focuses on the regulatory gap, not on inherent unsafety. The tone is analytical, not prohibitionist.
explanations.C: The passage states the scientific community responded with 'near-universal condemnation.' The author presents this consensus without defending the researcher's actions.
explanations.D: The passage's central argument is the 'gap between technical capability and the social infrastructure needed to govern it.' The author traces this gap through fragmented regulation, the CCR5 editing case, and the democratization of the technology. The tension is not that CRISPR is bad but that governance has not kept up.

questions.stem: The author describes the democratization of biotechnology as 'either the greatest promise of the CRISPR era or its greatest threat.' This framing most directly serves to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that community biohacking spaces should be shut down

choices.label: B
choices.text: support the Chinese researcher's decision to edit human embryos

choices.label: C
choices.text: demonstrate that CRISPR is too cheap to be valuable

choices.label: D
choices.text: illustrate the inherent ambiguity of technological progress, where the same development can be viewed as beneficial or dangerous depending on perspective

explanations.A: The author does not advocate closing biohacking spaces. The framing presents the democratization as ambiguous — potentially promising or threatening — without prescribing a specific policy response.
explanations.B: The democratization discussion is separate from the CCR5 case. The author uses the Chinese experiment to illustrate the governance gap, not to connect it to the democratization argument.
explanations.C: Cost reduction is presented as part of democratization, not as evidence that the technology lacks value. The passage treats affordability as increasing both opportunity and risk.
explanations.D: The 'either...or' construction explicitly frames the same development (cheap, accessible gene editing) as simultaneously promising and threatening. This rhetorical move captures the fundamental ambiguity of technological democratization: broader access enables innovation but also lowers barriers to misuse.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, the scientific community condemned the CCR5 editing experiment primarily because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the technology worked well enough to be applied in a context where safety, consent, and regulatory standards had not been met

choices.label: B
choices.text: HIV resistance was not a medically important goal

choices.label: C
choices.text: the experiment was too expensive and wasted research funding

choices.label: D
choices.text: the technology failed and the edits did not work as intended

explanations.A: The passage states the condemnation arose because the technology 'succeeded well enough to be dangerous in reckless hands.' The issue was not technical failure but premature application — using a powerful tool before the safety, ethical, and regulatory frameworks were in place.
explanations.B: HIV resistance is medically important, but the passage does not frame the condemnation around the goal's importance. The objection was about premature clinical application of germline editing.
explanations.C: Cost is not mentioned in connection with the CCR5 experiment. The condemnation was ethical and safety-related, not financial.
explanations.D: The passage explicitly states the condemnation was 'not because the technology failed but because it succeeded well enough to be dangerous in reckless hands.' The technology's functionality was the problem, not its failure.

questions.stem: The passage's description of U.S. regulatory fragmentation — FDA, EPA, and USDA with jurisdictions designed for earlier technologies — most directly supports which claim?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The U.S. has the most effective gene editing regulatory framework in the world

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fragmented regulation is always superior to centralized regulation because it provides more oversight

choices.label: C
choices.text: The FDA should have sole authority over all gene editing applications

choices.label: D
choices.text: Existing regulatory structures were not designed to address the specific challenges of gene editing technology

explanations.A: The passage presents U.S. regulation as fragmented and designed for earlier technologies — implying inadequacy, not effectiveness. It does not compare U.S. regulation favorably to other countries.
explanations.B: The passage presents fragmentation as a problem, not a strength. The author does not argue that multiple agencies provide better oversight — the framing suggests the opposite.
explanations.C: The passage does not recommend consolidating authority under the FDA or any single agency. It identifies fragmentation as a problem without prescribing a specific solution.
explanations.D: The passage states that U.S. agencies' jurisdictions 'were designed for earlier technologies,' directly supporting the claim that existing structures are not well-suited to gene editing. This illustrates the broader governance gap the author identifies.

questions.stem: Which of the following developments, if it occurred, would most weaken the author's central argument about the governance gap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Discovery of a new off-target effect of CRISPR that was previously unknown

choices.label: B
choices.text: A second case of unauthorized human germline editing in another country

choices.label: C
choices.text: A decrease in the cost of CRISPR reagents by 50%

choices.label: D
choices.text: An international treaty establishing binding, comprehensive, and adaptive regulatory standards for gene editing that are updated as the technology evolves

explanations.A: A new off-target effect would strengthen the author's argument by adding another dimension that governance must address. It does not close the governance gap.
explanations.B: A second unauthorized case would reinforce the author's point that governance is inadequate. It would strengthen the argument by providing additional evidence of the gap.
explanations.C: Lower costs would accelerate democratization and potentially widen the governance gap. This would strengthen, not weaken, the author's argument.
explanations.D: The author's argument is that regulatory and ethical frameworks have not kept pace with technical capability. An international treaty with binding, comprehensive, and adaptive standards would directly close this gap — demonstrating that governance can keep pace with technology. This would undermine the author's concern.

questions.passage:
The history of artificial intelligence research has been marked by cycles of inflated expectations followed by periods of disillusionment — a pattern researchers call 'AI winters.' In the 1960s, early pioneers predicted that machines would achieve human-level intelligence within twenty years. When progress stalled in the 1970s, funding dried up. The cycle repeated in the 1980s with expert systems: initial enthusiasm gave way to frustration when the systems proved brittle, unable to handle situations outside their narrow domains.

The current wave of AI, driven by deep learning and massive datasets, has achieved genuinely impressive results — superhuman performance in image recognition, language translation, and strategic games. Yet some researchers warn that the gap between narrow AI (systems that excel at specific tasks) and artificial general intelligence (AGI — systems with flexible, human-like reasoning across domains) is not a quantitative gap that more data and compute will close but a qualitative one requiring fundamentally different approaches.

The optimists counter that the history of technology is full of qualitative leaps that seemed impossible until they happened. The Wright brothers' airplane was not a better horse. The transition from narrow to general intelligence may similarly require a conceptual breakthrough rather than incremental improvement, but that does not make it impossible — only unpredictable.

What is certain is that the economic incentives driving AI research have never been stronger. Unlike previous AI booms, the current wave is sustained by immediate commercial applications worth hundreds of billions of dollars. Whether or not AGI arrives, narrow AI is already transforming industries. The question of whether this transformation constitutes genuine progress toward machine intelligence or merely sophisticated pattern matching is perhaps the defining scientific debate of the era.
questions.stem: The author characterizes the debate between AI optimists and skeptics as primarily a disagreement about:

choices.label: A
choices.text: whether current AI systems have any practical commercial value

choices.label: B
choices.text: whether the gap between narrow AI and AGI is quantitative (closable with more resources) or qualitative (requiring fundamentally different approaches)

choices.label: C
choices.text: whether the Wright brothers' airplane was a significant invention

choices.label: D
choices.text: whether previous AI winters were caused by insufficient funding

explanations.A: Both sides acknowledge the commercial value of current AI. The passage states narrow AI is 'already transforming industries.' The debate is about the path to AGI, not about commercial value.
explanations.B: The passage frames the core disagreement: skeptics see a 'qualitative' gap requiring 'fundamentally different approaches,' while optimists counter that technology history includes 'qualitative leaps that seemed impossible until they happened.' The debate is about the nature of the gap, not whether current systems are useful.
explanations.C: The airplane analogy illustrates the optimists' point about qualitative leaps but is not itself the subject of the debate.
explanations.D: Previous AI winters and their causes provide historical context but are not the focus of the current optimist-skeptic disagreement.

questions.stem: The author's reference to 'AI winters' serves primarily to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that the current AI boom will inevitably end in another period of disillusionment

choices.label: B
choices.text: provide historical context showing a recurring pattern of overexpectation, suggesting caution about current claims while acknowledging that this wave may differ

choices.label: C
choices.text: demonstrate that AI research has never produced useful results

choices.label: D
choices.text: criticize the researchers of the 1960s for making irresponsible predictions

explanations.A: The author does not predict another AI winter. The passage notes that current economic incentives are stronger than ever, suggesting this wave may differ. The historical pattern provides context for caution, not prediction.
explanations.B: The AI winters illustrate a recurring pattern of inflated expectations followed by disillusionment. By establishing this pattern, the author contextualizes the current debate: current achievements are impressive, but history teaches that enthusiasm should be tempered. The passage acknowledges that 'unlike previous AI booms, the current wave is sustained by immediate commercial applications,' suggesting this cycle may be different.
explanations.C: The passage explicitly states that current AI has achieved 'genuinely impressive results' including 'superhuman performance.' Previous expert systems also produced useful (if limited) results.
explanations.D: The author presents the 1960s predictions as overconfident but does not criticize the researchers' character or responsibility. The tone is analytical, not accusatory.

questions.stem: The author describes the current transformation by narrow AI as raising the question of whether it constitutes 'genuine progress toward machine intelligence or merely sophisticated pattern matching.' This characterization suggests the author:

choices.label: A
choices.text: is firmly convinced that current AI is merely pattern matching with no scientific significance

choices.label: B
choices.text: believes the answer to this question is obvious and not worthy of serious debate

choices.label: C
choices.text: views this as a genuinely open question whose answer has profound implications for how we understand intelligence itself

choices.label: D
choices.text: is primarily concerned about the economic impact of AI on employment

explanations.A: The author presents this as 'perhaps the defining scientific debate of the era,' indicating genuine uncertainty rather than firm conviction. Calling something a defining debate implies it is unresolved.
explanations.B: Calling it the 'defining scientific debate of the era' indicates the author takes the question very seriously. Defining debates are by definition worthy of serious engagement.
explanations.C: By framing the question as 'the defining scientific debate of the era,' the author signals that the answer is genuinely uncertain and has broad implications. Whether narrow AI's achievements represent progress toward general intelligence or are qualitatively different from it affects how we understand the nature of intelligence, the prospects for AGI, and the significance of current AI systems.
explanations.D: While economic impact is mentioned (commercial applications worth hundreds of billions), the author frames the central question as scientific and philosophical (the nature of intelligence), not primarily economic.

questions.stem: The Wright brothers' airplane analogy is used by AI optimists to argue that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: airplanes and computers are fundamentally similar technologies

choices.label: B
choices.text: qualitative technological breakthroughs can occur even when the path from current capabilities to the breakthrough is not visible in advance

choices.label: C
choices.text: incremental improvement of narrow AI systems will inevitably lead to AGI

choices.label: D
choices.text: the history of aviation proves that AGI will arrive within twenty years

explanations.A: The passage does not argue that airplanes and computers are similar technologies. The analogy is structural, not substantive: it illustrates how qualitative leaps happen in technology, not that the specific technologies are related.
explanations.B: The passage states that 'the Wright brothers' airplane was not a better horse' — it was a qualitatively different approach to transportation. The optimists use this to argue that the transition to AGI may similarly require a conceptual breakthrough rather than incremental improvement, and that such breakthroughs can happen even though they are 'unpredictable' in advance.
explanations.C: The optimists explicitly do not argue for incremental improvement. The airplane analogy is about qualitative leaps, not gradual progress. An airplane is a conceptual breakthrough, not a faster horse.
explanations.D: The analogy does not include a timeline prediction. The optimists argue that qualitative leaps are possible and unpredictable, not that they are imminent.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the skeptics' position that the narrow-to-general AI gap is qualitative rather than quantitative?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A study showing that the largest language models, despite scaling to trillions of parameters, consistently fail at simple reasoning tasks that require understanding causality rather than statistical correlation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A report that AI companies have increased their computing budgets by 500% over the past year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that a new deep learning architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance on an image recognition benchmark

choices.label: D
choices.text: A survey showing that most AI researchers are optimistic about achieving AGI within 30 years

explanations.A: If massive scaling (more data, more compute, more parameters) fails to produce even basic causal reasoning — a hallmark of general intelligence — this directly supports the claim that the gap is qualitative, not quantitative. More of the same approach does not close the gap, suggesting fundamentally different methods are needed.
explanations.B: Increased computing budgets suggest continued investment but do not address whether the gap is qualitative. More spending on the same approach is consistent with both positions.
explanations.C: Better performance on a narrow benchmark supports the quantitative view (more scale = better results) and does not address whether the approach can generalize to AGI.
explanations.D: Researcher optimism is a sociological finding, not evidence about the nature of the gap. Previous generations of researchers were also optimistic before AI winters.

questions.passage:
The microbiome — the vast community of trillions of microorganisms inhabiting the human body — has emerged as one of the most fertile areas of biomedical research. Studies have linked gut microbiome composition to conditions ranging from inflammatory bowel disease and obesity to depression and autoimmune disorders. The sheer volume of associations reported in the literature has created a sense that the microbiome influences virtually every aspect of human health.

Skeptics caution that association is not causation. Many microbiome studies are cross-sectional: they compare the gut bacteria of people with a disease to those without and find differences. But these differences could be consequences of the disease (altered diet, medication, immune response) rather than causes. The few studies that have attempted to establish causality — typically through fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) in animal models — face the criticism that gnotobiotic mice (mice raised germ-free) are profoundly abnormal in ways that may not generalize to conventional animals or humans.

Moreover, the field has been plagued by reproducibility problems. Different DNA sequencing methods, bioinformatic pipelines, and sample collection protocols can produce dramatically different results from the same biological samples. What looks like a disease-associated microbial signature in one laboratory may not replicate in another.

None of this means the microbiome is unimportant. FMT for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection — one of the few clinical applications with rigorous evidence — has cure rates exceeding 90%, demonstrating that microbial community composition can be decisively important in at least some clinical contexts. The challenge is distinguishing the handful of robust, causal relationships from the hundreds of intriguing but unreliable correlations.
questions.stem: The author's overall stance toward microbiome research can best be described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: enthusiastic and uncritical — the microbiome affects virtually every aspect of health

choices.label: B
choices.text: dismissive — microbiome research has produced no valuable clinical insights

choices.label: C
choices.text: cautiously optimistic — acknowledging genuine clinical successes while warning against overinterpreting correlational evidence

choices.label: D
choices.text: neutral to the point of offering no evaluative judgment on the field

explanations.A: The author explicitly warns against the uncritical view. The passage criticizes cross-sectional designs, reproducibility problems, and the association-causation conflation. 'Enthusiastic and uncritical' contradicts the passage's cautionary tone.
explanations.B: The author explicitly acknowledges FMT for C. difficile as a genuine clinical success with 'cure rates exceeding 90%.' Calling the field's insights valueless contradicts this.
explanations.C: The author balances criticism (cross-sectional designs, reproducibility issues, animal model limitations) with genuine acknowledgment of success (FMT for C. difficile, recognition that the microbiome 'can be decisively important'). The concluding sentence frames the challenge as separating robust findings from unreliable correlations — a cautiously optimistic stance.
explanations.D: The author makes clear evaluative judgments: correlational studies are criticized, reproducibility problems are flagged, and FMT for C. difficile is praised. This is not neutral.

questions.stem: The author raises the concern about gnotobiotic mice to illustrate which methodological limitation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Animal models may not faithfully recapitulate human disease mechanisms because germ-free mice develop abnormally, limiting the generalizability of causal claims made from FMT experiments in these models

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mice are too small to provide sufficient fecal samples for microbiome analysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gnotobiotic mice are too expensive for most research laboratories to maintain

choices.label: D
choices.text: FMT experiments in mice have never produced any positive results

explanations.A: Gnotobiotic (germ-free) mice are 'profoundly abnormal' — they have underdeveloped immune systems, altered gut morphology, and atypical metabolism. When researchers colonize these mice with human microbiota and observe effects, the results may reflect the abnormal baseline rather than a genuine causal relationship. The author uses this to question whether animal model results generalize to normal humans.
explanations.B: Sample size is not the concern raised. The author's criticism is about the biological abnormality of germ-free mice, not about practical constraints of sample collection.
explanations.C: Cost is not mentioned as a concern. The methodological limitation is biological validity, not economic feasibility.
explanations.D: The author does not claim FMT experiments in mice have failed. The concern is about whether positive results in abnormal animals generalize to normal physiology.

questions.stem: The author's discussion of FMT for C. difficile infection serves primarily to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that FMT should be used to treat all microbiome-related diseases

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrate that microbiome interventions can work, preventing the critique from being read as a wholesale dismissal of the field

choices.label: C
choices.text: suggest that C. difficile is the only disease influenced by the microbiome

choices.label: D
choices.text: criticize the FDA for not approving more microbiome-based therapies

explanations.A: The author presents FMT for C. difficile as a specific success, not as evidence that FMT should be broadly applied. The passage's central point is that most microbiome-disease associations lack the causal evidence that the C. difficile case provides.
explanations.B: By including the C. difficile example immediately after extensive criticism, the author prevents the passage from reading as a complete dismissal. The 90% cure rate demonstrates that microbial communities can be 'decisively important,' establishing that the critique is about methodological rigor, not about whether the microbiome matters.
explanations.C: The author states the microbiome 'can be decisively important in at least some clinical contexts,' implying other robust associations may exist. C. difficile is highlighted as the strongest current example, not the only one.
explanations.D: FDA approval is not discussed in the passage. The author's focus is on the quality of evidence, not regulatory policy.

questions.stem: A new large-scale microbiome study uses longitudinal sampling (following the same patients over time), standardized sequencing protocols across all sites, and shows that changes in a specific bacterial species precede the onset of inflammatory bowel disease by 6-12 months. This study would:

choices.label: A
choices.text: address several of the author's methodological concerns — cross-sectional design, reproducibility, and temporal ordering — while still falling short of definitive causation

choices.label: B
choices.text: definitively prove that the bacterial species causes inflammatory bowel disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: be irrelevant to the author's argument because animal models are needed for all causal claims

choices.label: D
choices.text: weaken the C. difficile FMT evidence by suggesting that a different approach is needed

explanations.A: Longitudinal design addresses the cross-sectional limitation (it establishes temporal ordering — bacterial changes precede disease). Standardized protocols address reproducibility. However, even longitudinal correlation does not establish causation (a confound could cause both the microbial change and the disease). The study significantly strengthens the evidence but does not reach the level of a randomized interventional trial.
explanations.B: Temporal precedence is necessary but not sufficient for causation. A confounding variable could cause both the microbial change and the disease. Hill's criteria for causation require additional evidence: dose-response, biological plausibility, experimental manipulation, etc.
explanations.C: The author does not claim animal models are required for all causal claims. Human longitudinal studies and interventional trials (like FMT for C. difficile) can also provide causal evidence.
explanations.D: The study is about a different disease (IBD vs. C. difficile) and does not affect the C. difficile evidence. Different diseases may have different relationships with the microbiome.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the author's view of the relationship between association and causation in microbiome research?

choices.label: A
choices.text: All associations will eventually be proven causal with sufficient research funding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Associations are scientifically worthless and should not be published

choices.label: C
choices.text: Associations are useful starting points for generating hypotheses, but the field has been too quick to treat them as established causal relationships

choices.label: D
choices.text: Causation can never be established in biological research, so associations are the best available evidence

explanations.A: The author's entire argument is that most associations may not be causal — they could reflect consequences of disease, confounds, or methodological artifacts. More funding does not automatically convert correlations into causal relationships.
explanations.B: The author does not dismiss associations as worthless. The passage frames the challenge as 'distinguishing the handful of robust, causal relationships from the hundreds of intriguing but unreliable correlations' — implying that associations have value as leads, not that they should be suppressed.
explanations.C: The passage's critical analysis targets the premature acceptance of correlations as causal. The author values the C. difficile FMT evidence precisely because it goes beyond correlation. The message is methodological: associations generate hypotheses, but causal claims require interventional evidence.
explanations.D: The C. difficile FMT example demonstrates that causation can be established (90% cure rate from microbial intervention). The author does not adopt an epistemological position that causation is unknowable.
